elements.sent-2411-001: Completely positive trace-preserving maps $S$, also known as quantum channels, arise in quantum physics as a description of how the density operator $\rho$ of a system changes in a given time interval, allowing not only for unitary evolution but arbitrary operations including measurements or other interaction with an environment.
elements.sent-2411-002: It is known that if the Hilbert space $\Hilbert$ that $\rho$ acts on is finite-dimensional, then every $S$ must have a fixed point, i.e., a density operator $\rho_0$ with $S(\rho_0)=\rho_0$.
elements.sent-2411-003: In infinite dimension, $S$ need not have a fixed point in general.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, we prove here the existence of a fixed point under a certain additional assumption which is, roughly speaking, that $S$ leaves invariant a certain set of density operators with bounded ``cost'' of preparation.
elements.sent-2411-005: The proof is an application of the Schauder-Tychonoff fixed point theorem.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our motivation for this question comes from a proposal of Deutsch for how to define quantum theory in a space-time with closed timelike curves; our result supports the viability of Deutsch's proposal.
elements.sent-2411-007: Key words: Quantum channels; Retrocausality; Closed timelike curves; Open Quantum Systems; Discrete Time.
elements.sent-2411-008: We present here a sufficient condition for the existence of fixed points of completely positive trace-preserving maps.
elements.sent-2411-009: Completely positive trace-preserving (CPTP) maps, also known as quantum channels, arise in quantum theory as super-operators, i.e., mapping density operators to density operators rather than state vectors to state vectors.
elements.sent-2411-010: They represent the time evolution, say from time $0$ to time $T$, also in cases in which the time evolution is not unitary, such as cases given by a Lindblad equation, or involving quantum measurements, or open systems in general.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our motivation for the present paper comes from the discussion of whether quantum theory can be defined in a relativistic background space-time with closed timelike curves (CTCs), specifically from a proposal by David Deutsch \cite{deutsch_quantum_1991 in which a CPTP map is associated with going around a CTC once, and the existence of a fixed point amounts to the consistency of the theory.}
elements.sent-2411-012: We discuss Deutsch's proposal and the application of our result to it in Section \ref{section3 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Quantum channels are also considered in quantum information theory, in particular as models of noisy systems, where they are typically assumed to act on finite dimensional Hilbert spaces.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, also there it may be of interest to consider an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space, either if it is the fundamentally correct one or if it provides a model that is simpler in some other respect (e.g., by obeying symmetries or being a bosonic Fock space).
elements.sent-2411-015: Our result, formulated precisely as Theorem \ref{thm1 in Section \ref{section2 , provides a sufficient condition for the existence of a fixed point of a CPTP map $S$.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The condition is expressed in terms of several commuting positive self-adjoint operators $A_1,\ldots, A_m$; for these, we have in mind that states with higher values of these observables are more ``costly'' to prepare.
elements.sent-2411-017: For example, the $A_i$ could be particle number or kinetic energy, as more resources (``cost'') are required to prepare states with higher particle number or energy.
elements.sent-2411-018: We consider a set $K$ of density operators for which, intuitively speaking, the average cost is limited; more precisely, we consider an axiparallel hyper-rectangle $B$ in $\mathbb{R ^m$ (which intuitively speaking contains the allowed, limited, average values of $A_1,\ldots,A_m$) and define $K$ to be the set of density operators for which the averages of $A_1,\ldots,A_m$ lie in $B$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Our condition is that $S$ maps $K$ into itself, $S(K)\subseteq K$.
elements.sent-2411-020: The proof of our theorem is based on the Schauder-Tychonoff fixed point theorem, which states that for a nonempty, convex, and compact set $K$ in a locally convex space $X$ and a continuous mapping $S:K\to K$, there exists a fixed point $p\in K$, i.e., $S(p)=p$ (Theorem 5.28 in \cite{rudin_functional_1991 ).}
elements.sent-2411-021: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-022: In Section \ref{section2 , we formulate our main result, Theorem \ref{thm1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In Section \ref{section3 , we describe Deutsch's proposal for defining a quantum theory on a curved space-time with CTCs and explain what our theorem entails for it.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We begin with some definitions to recap the concepts of being completely positive and trace preserving: Let $\Hilbert$ be a separable Hilbert space (i.e., one that possesses a countably infinite or finite orthonormal basis) and let $T(\Hilbert)$ be the trace class of $\Hilbert$ (the set of operators $\rho:\Hilbert\to\Hilbert$ with $\Tr(\sqrt{\rho \rho^\dag )<\infty$).}
elements.sent-2411-026: By a super-operator, we mean a linear mapping $S:T(\Hilbert)\to T(\Hilbert)$ that is bounded in the trace norm \begin{equation}\label{trace norm definition} \norm{\rho}_1 \coloneqq \Tr(\sqrt{\rho \rho^\dag}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-027: A completely positive, trace-preserving super-operator is also called a CPTP map or a quantum channel.
elements.sent-2411-028: We denote the set of density operators, i.e., the set of all positive self-adjoint linear operators of trace 1 on a Hilbert space $\Hilbert$ by $D(\Hilbert)$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Note that every quantum channel maps $D(\Hilbert)$ to itself.
elements.sent-2411-030: Two unbounded self-adjoint operators $A_1$ and $A_2$ are said to commute if all spectral projections of the two operators commute.
elements.sent-2411-031: A set of unbounded self-adjoint operators is said to commute if they all commute pairwise.
elements.sent-2411-032: Let $A_1, \hdots, A_m$ be commuting, positive possibly unbounded self-adjoint operators on $\Hilbert$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Let $B \subset \mathbb{R ^m$ be a hyper-rectangle of the form $B\coloneqq [0,b_1]\times \hdots \times [0,b_m]$ for positive numbers $b_1,\hdots, b_m \in (0,\infty)$ and set \begin{equation}\label{Klabel} K \coloneqq \{\rho \in D(\Hilbert)|\: \rho A_i \in T(\Hilbert) \: \forall i, \, (\Tr(\rho A_1), \hdots, \Tr(\rho A_m))\in B \}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{Klabel} K \coloneqq \{\rho \in D(\Hilbert)|\: \rho A_i \in T(\Hilbert) \: \forall i, \, (\Tr(\rho A_1), \hdots, \Tr(\rho A_m))\in B \}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-035: Suppose also that additionally for all choices of $m$ non-negative numbers $a_1, \hdots , a_m \in \mathbb{R}$ with $0\leq a_i <\infty $ $\forall i\in \{1,\hdots,m\}$, \begin{equation}\label{secondCondition} \dim(\Hilbert_1 \cap \hdots \cap \Hilbert_m)<\infty,\end{equation} where $\Hilbert_i$ denotes the spectral subspace of $A_i$ for the interval $[0,a_i]$.
elements.sent-2411-036: Then either $K=\emptyset$ or there exists a density operator $\rho \in K \: \text{such that \: S(\rho)=\rho$.. \end{thm.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The conditions \eqref{Klabel and \eqref{secondCondition of Theorem \ref{thm1 can be equivalently rephrased in terms of a projection valued measure (PVM) $P(\omega)$ on the Borel sigma algebra of $\mathbb{R ^m$: From the PVM we can define the commuting positive self-adjoint operators $A_i$ as \begin{equation}\label{Aiswerdendefiniert} A_i=\int_{\mathbb{R}_+^m} P(d\Vec{x}) x_i\end{equation} where $\mathbb{R _+=[0,\infty)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Conversely, by the spectral theorem we know of the unique existence of a PVM jointly diagonalizing the given $A_i$ (\cite{reed_methods_1980 , Chapter VIII.3).}
elements.sent-2411-039: Using these relations, Theorem \ref{thm1 can equivalently be stated as: Let $\Hilbert$ be a separable Hilbert space, $S: T(\Hilbert) \to T(\Hilbert)$ a quantum channel, and $P(\omega)$ a PVM on the Borel sigma algebra of $\mathbb{R^m$ supported only on the positive octant $\mathbb{R _+^m$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Let $B \subset \mathbb{R ^m$ be a hyper-rectangle of the form $B\coloneqq [0,b_1]\times \hdots \times [0,b_m]$ for positive numbers $b_1,\hdots, b_m \in (0,\infty)$ and set \begin{equation}\label{KdefinitionPVM} K \coloneqq \Biggl\{\rho \in D(\Hilbert) \Bigg| \int_{\mathbb{R}_+^m} \Tr(\rho P(d\Vec{x}))\Vec{x} \in B \Biggr\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Suppose also that additionally for all choices of $m$ non-negative numbers $a_1, \hdots , a_m \in \mathbb{R}$ with $0\leq a_i <\infty $ $\forall i\in \{1,\hdots,m\}$, \begin{equation}\Tr \left(P([0, a_1]\times \hdots \times [0, a_m])\right) <\infty.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-042: Then either $K=\emptyset$ or there exists a density operator $\rho \in K \: \text{such that \: S(\rho)=\rho$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Note that $\rho P(M) \in T(\Hilbert)$ for every set $M$ because $P(M)$ is bounded and the product of a trace-class operator and a bounded operator always lies in the trace class (\cite{reed_methods_1980 , Thm.. IV.19(b)).}
elements.sent-2411-044: Also note and that $\int_{\mathbb{R ^m_+ \Tr(\rho P(d\Vec{x ))x_i < \infty$ implies $\rho A_i \in T(\Hilbert)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Since $\rho$ and $A$ are self-adjoint, $|\rho A_i| = \sqrt{\rho A_i A_i \rho = \rho^{1/2 |A_i| \rho^{1/2}$; since $A_i$ is positive, $|A_i|=A_i$ and $|\rho A_i| = \int_{\mathbb{R _+^m \rho^{1/2 P(d\vec{x ) \rho^{1/2 x_i$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-047: Theorem \ref{thm1 can be slightly generalized with respect to the allowed set $B \subset \mathbb{R ^m$: It is also valid for a compact convex $B\subset \mathbb{R ^m_+$ with the additional requirement that for all $(x_1, \hdots, x_m) \in B$ the elements $(0, x_2 \hdots, x_m),\, (x_1, 0, x_3 \hdots, x_m), \hdots,\, (x_1, \hdots, x_{m-1 , 0)$ are also elements of $B$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Let $\Hilbert$ be a finite dimensional Hilbert space and $S: T(\Hilbert) \to T(\Hilbert)$ a quantum channel.
elements.sent-2411-049: Then there exists a fixed point $\rho \in D(\Hilbert)$ of $S$, i.e., $S(\rho)=\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-050: That is, for a finite dimensional Hilbert space, the existence of a fixed point of any quantum channel follows even without the additional restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-051: This can be deduced from elementary considerations \cite{deutsch_quantum_1991 which we include here for the reader's convenience: Notice first that set of all density operators on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space is compact (as it is a closed bounded set in the finite-dimensional space $T(\Hilbert)$).}
elements.sent-2411-052: Consider now the expression \begin{equation}\label{converges} {\rho}(N)=\frac{1}{N+1}\sum^N_{n=0} S^n ( {\rho}(0)),\end{equation} where $ {\rho (0)$ is any density operator; $ {\rho (N)$ is the mean of $N+1$ density operators and therefore itself a density operator.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Since \begin{equation}S(\rho(N))-\rho(N)=\frac{1}{N+1} (S^{N+1}(\rho(0))-\rho(0)),\end{equation} because the left hand side is a telescopic sum of the right hand side, the trace norm of $S({\rho (N))- {\rho (N)$ obeys \begin{equation}\label{Deutschproof} 0 \leq \norm{S( {\rho}(N))- {\rho}(N)}_1 \leq \frac{2}{N+1} .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The theorem of Bolzano-Weierstraß states that any bounded sequence in a finite-dimensional vector space has at least one accumulation point \cite{konigsberger_analysis_2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Thus, the sequence $ {\rho (N)$, which lies in the bounded set $D(\Hilbert)$, must have an accumulation point $ {\rho _{ac}$, so $\rho_{ac =\lim_{k\to\infty \rho(N_k) $ for some subsequence $N_k \to \infty$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Applying \eqref{Deutschproof to $N_k$, we find that $S(\rho(N_k))\to \rho_{ac $ as $k\to \infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: By continuity of $S$, \begin{equation}S(\rho_{ac})=S(\lim_{k\to\infty} \rho(N_k))=\lim_{k\to\infty} S(\rho(N_k))=\rho_{ac},\end{equation} so $\rho_{ac}$ is a fixed point of $S$, and the claim is proved.\footnote{Using Brouwer's fixed-point theorem, an alternative proof is possible \cite{wolf_quantum_2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Brouwer's fixed-point theorem states that any continuous function $f$ mapping a nonempty compact convex set in a finite-dimensional vector space to itself, there is a point $x_0$ in that set such that $f(x_0)=x_0$ \cite{ben-el-mechaieh_elementary_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: The quantum channel $S$ is a linear operator mapping the set $D(\Hilbert)$ to itself.
elements.sent-2411-060: Because $S$ is linear, it is also continuous.
elements.sent-2411-061: The set of all density operators acting on $\Hilbert$ is non-empty, compact and also convex.
elements.sent-2411-062: Therefore, there exists at least one fixed-point ${\rho _0 \in D(\Hilbert)$ such that $S({\rho _0)={\rho _0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The sequence $\rho(N)$ in \eqref{converges does not only have a convergent subsequence, but actually converges for any initial $\rho(0) \in D(\Hilbert)$ (Chapter 6 in \cite{wolf_quantum_2012 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-064: In infinite dimensions a quantum channel can fail to have a fixed point.
elements.sent-2411-065: Here is an example: let $\Hilbert=\ell^2 = L^2(\mathbb{N )$ be the space of square-summable sequences and $V$ the right shift, \begin{equation}V(x_1,x_2,\hdots)=V(0,x_1,x_2,\hdots).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Then the map $S(\rho)=V\rho V^\dag$ is a quantum channel without a fixed point.

elements.sent-2411-075: In this section we explain the relevance of fixed point theorems, in particular Theorem \ref{thm1 , to quantum mechanics on space-times with causality violations, in particular with closed timelike curves (CTCs).}
elements.sent-2411-076: While we do not claim that CTCs exist in reality, we are interested in the theoretical challenge of whether and how quantum mechanics can be formulated on a space-time with CTCs.
elements.sent-2411-077: As we explain below, the strategy proposed for this purpose by Deutsch \cite{deutsch_quantum_1991 seems reasonable and viable, even more so in the light of Theorem \ref{thm1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The framework of general relativity, in which space-time is a Lorentzian manifold, in principle allows for CTCs.
elements.sent-2411-079: Explicit metrics with CTCs have come up in various contexts \cite{godel_example_1949, thorne_closed_1993, bonnor_exact_2005, carter_Kerr , and there is considerable literature on conditions under which the Einstein field equation of general relativity would lead to the formation of CTCs, or conditions that would preclude such solutions (e.g., \cite{friedman_cauchy_1990, kim_vacuum_1991 ), or whether those CTCs would be traversable.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Other kinds of retrocausality have also been studied, for example in classical electrodynamics in flat space-time by Wheeler and Feynman \cite{wheeler_classical_1949 .}
elements.sent-2411-081: The obvious problem arising from space-times with CTCs is that if a particle were to traverse a CTC, it would encounter its own past.
elements.sent-2411-082: The following causality paradox is often called the grandfather paradox and has a long history: A person has figured out how to time travel.
elements.sent-2411-083: The person goes back in time and kills his grandfather.
elements.sent-2411-084: Therefore the time traveler is never born---a contradiction.
elements.sent-2411-085: We can also formulate an analogous paradox for a single particle: Consider a scenario where a particle, following a CTC, collides with its younger self, altering its trajectory so that its younger self now follows a different path.
elements.sent-2411-086: Following that path, however, the particle will later fail to collide with its younger self, yielding a contradiction.
elements.sent-2411-087: It thus seems that the existence and traversability of CTCs leads to logical inconsistencies and thus precludes the possibility of physical theories, classical or quantum, on such a space-time.
elements.sent-2411-088: Some authors \cite{arntzenius_time_2002 have argued that the laws of physics will still have sufficiently many consistent solutions to allow for a plausible course of events avoiding any contradiction.}
elements.sent-2411-089: We now turn to a particular way out of the paradox for quantum theories proposed by Deutsch \cite{deutsch_quantum_1991 .}
elements.sent-2411-090: The simplest toy example $\mathscr{M _1$ of a space-time with CTCs is obtained from Minkowski space-time by ``rolling it up'': consider the slab between two parallel spacelike 3-planes $\mathcal{P _1,\mathcal{P _2$, and identify $\mathcal{P _1$ with $\mathcal{P _2$ along a time translation $\tau$ that maps $\mathcal{P _1$ to $\mathcal{P _2$; that is, a future-directed timelike current hitting the past side of $\mathcal{P _2$ at $x$ comes out from the future side of $\mathcal{P _1$ at $\tau^{-1 (x)$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: In a Lorentz frame in which $\mathcal{P _1$ and $\mathcal{P _2$ are surfaces of constant time coordinate and $\tau$ translates along the time axis (see Figure \ref{fig:Sketchoftimecylinder ), $\mathcal{M _1$ can be written as \begin{equation}\left( \mathbb{R}_{/T\mathbb{Z}} \times \mathbb{R}^3 , ds^2=- dt^2+ dx_1^2+dx_2^2+dx_3^2\right)\end{equation} where $T>0$ is the timelike distance between $\mathcal{P _1$ and $\mathcal{P _2$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The symbol $\mathbb{R _{/T\mathbb{Z }$ means that we are considering the set $[0,T)$ where the upper end is glued to the lower end, i.e., $T$ is identified with zero.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Topologically this corresponds to a circle $\mathbb{S ^1$, so the $t$ coordinate can now be regarded as cyclic.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale = 1]{Figure1.png} \caption{Space-time diagram of the simple causality violating space-time $\mathscr{M}_1$} \label{fig:Sketchoftimecylinder}
elements.sent-2411-094: Considering wave functions on $\mathscr{M _1$ leads to the following problem: Since $\mathcal{P _2$ is really the same set of space-time points as $\mathcal{P _1$, the wave function on $\mathcal{P _2$ should agree with that on $\mathcal{P _1$; or in coordinates, $\psi(t=T)=\psi(t=0)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: However, for a unitary time evolution operator $e^{-iHT/\hbar $ representing the time evolution for time length T, the equation \begin{equation}\ket{\Psi}= e^{-iHT/\hbar} \ket{\Psi}\end{equation} does not necessarily have a solution $\ket{\Psi}\in L^2(\mathbb{R ^3)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: It only has a solution if $H$ has an eigenvalue of $ \frac{2\pi \hbar {T k$ for a $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: For generic $H$, this is not the case (and even if such eigenvalues exist, the solutions $\ket{\Psi}$ constrained in this way are presumably too few for an acceptable physical theory).
elements.sent-2411-098: For density operators the situation is different.
elements.sent-2411-099: Any $\rho$ diagonal in an eigenbasis of the Hamiltonian $H$ governing the time evolution is invariant under the time evolution.
elements.sent-2411-100: Therefore it is the same after a circumnavigation of the time cylinder described above.
elements.sent-2411-101: Having such $\rho$ is therefore logically consistent with no constraints on the Hamiltonian or the length of the time cylinder.
elements.sent-2411-102: Thus, the crucial problem of the consistency of the theory in the presence of closed timelike curves can be, it seems, much improved if we are willing to contemplate the possibility that the quantum state of the universe is fundamentally mixed---not because of the observers' ignorance of the actual wave function, and not either because of tracing out some degrees of freedom, but directly on the fundamental level.
elements.sent-2411-103: This possibility, that the fundamental quantum state may not be a wave function but a density operator, has been considered before for other reasons under the name ``density matrix realism'' \cite{durr_role_2005, Chen25 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: To obtain a more nuanced model of CTCs allowing for a causality violating region as well as a chronology respecting region we modify a Minkowski space-time to obtain a space time $\mathscr{M _2$ in the following way: We choose a space-volume $\mathcal{F}\subset \mathbb{R ^3$ and two times $T_1$ and $T_2$ with $T_1<T_2$ to obtain two spacelike sets \begin{equation}\mathcal{F}_i\coloneqq \{T_i\} \times \mathcal{F}\end{equation} We then identify $\mathcal{F _1$ with $\mathcal{F _2$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: A sketch is given in Figure \ref{fig:SketchofHilbertSpaces .}
elements.sent-2411-106: This model can incorporate CTCs because it transports whole states from one space-time surface at time $T_2$ to the other at the earlier time $T_1$.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale = 0.7]{Figure2.png} \caption{Space-time diagram of Deutsch's model space-time $\mathscr{M}_2$ with the chronology respecting region to the past of $T_1$, the chronology respecting region to the future of $T_2$ and the region with CTCs between $T_1$ and $T_2$} \label{fig:SketchofHilbertSpaces}
elements.sent-2411-107: The normal unitary evolution applies in the chronology respecting regions prior to $T_1$ and after $T_2$.

elements.sent-2411-110: We postulate first that the density operator $\rho_{T_1+}$ (right after time $T_1$) on $\mathscr{H _{\mathrm{full }$ arises from a given $\rho_{T_1-}$ (right before $T_1$) by combining a state $\rho_1$ coming out of the future side of $\mathcal{F _1$ with the prior reduced state, \begin{equation}\rho_{in}:=\Tr_{\mathcal{F}}(\rho_{T_1-})\end{equation} on $\mathcal{F ^c$, which we call the initial state from the chronology respecting region.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-112: We postulate next that $\rho$ evolves unitarily between $T_1+$ and $T_2-$, \begin{equation}\rho_{T_2-}= U \, \rho_{T_1+} \, U^\dagger\end{equation} with $U=e^{-iH(T_2-T_1)/\hbar}$.
elements.sent-2411-113: Finally, we postulate that the reduced state \begin{equation}\rho_2:=\Tr_{\mathcal{F}^c}(\rho_{T_2-})\end{equation} on $\mathcal{F}$ gets transported back from $\mathcal{F _2$ to $\mathcal{F _1$.\footnote{For the region after $T_2$, we may postulate, e.g., $\rho_{T_2+ = \Tr_{\mathcal{F (\rho_{T_2- ) \otimes |0\rangle \langle 0|$ (with $|0\rangle$ the Fock vacuum vector) or $\rho_{T_2+ = \Tr_{\mathcal{F (\rho_{T_2- ) \otimes \Tr_{\mathcal{F ^c (\rho_{T_1- )$, but that does not matter for the considerations in this paper.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Consistency of the time evolution then amounts to \begin{equation}\rho_2 = \rho_1\end{equation} or \begin{equation}S(\rho_1)=\rho_1\end{equation} with $S$ given by \begin{equation}\label{quantumchannel} S(\rho) = \Tr_{in} (U \, \rho_{in} \otimes \rho \, U^\dagger) \,,\end{equation} where $\Tr_{in}$ means the same as $\Tr_{\mathcal{F ^c}$, i.e., a partial trace over the initial degrees of freedom from the chronology respecting region.}
elements.sent-2411-115: For given $\rho_{in}$, the mapping $S$ represents the time evolution from $\mathcal{F _1$ to $\mathcal{F _2$ and back, and thus going around the CTC once.}}
elements.sent-2411-116: The consistency of the postulated dynamics is equivalent to the existence of fixed points of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-117: As mentioned before, for the case of finite dimensional quantum mechanics, such a point exists for every $\rho_{in}\in \Hilbert_{\mathcal{F ^c}$.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Let $\Hilbert_{\mathcal{F^c}$ and $\Hilbert_{\mathcal{F }$ both be separable Hilbert spaces.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Then the map \eqref{quantumchannel is a quantum channel.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Indeed, $S$ is trace preserving: Let $\rho \in T(\Hilbert)$ be a trace class operator on $\Hilbert$, then \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Tr(S(\rho)) =\Tr(\Tr_{in}(U{\rho}_{in}\otimes {\rho}U^\dag)) =\Tr(U{\rho}_{in}\otimes {\rho}U^\dag) =\Tr({\rho}_{in}\otimes {\rho})= \Tr(\rho_{in})\Tr(\rho)=\Tr(\rho) \end{split}\end{equation} as $\rho_{in}$ has trace 1.

elements.sent-2411-129: Essentially, these conditions say that the average particle number and energy cannot be increased without limits through iterations of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-130: They seem plausible if we imagine that even for a time machine it would be too good to be true if we could arbitrarily multiply resources (such as particles and energy) by letting them repeatedly traverse a CTC.\footnote{For example, it seems plausible that high pressure in the rectangle $[T_1,T_2]\times \mathcal{F}$ between $\mathcal{F _1$ and $\mathcal{F _2$ in Figure \ref{fig:SketchofHilbertSpaces leads to the expulsion of particles from this rectangle.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Furthermore, it would seem that the space-time is unstable if energy can accumulate and increase from seemingly nowhere; after all, by the Einstein equation this large amount of energy would cause a change of the space-time geometry, perhaps even removing the CTCs.

elements.sent-2411-167: Part 3: $K$ is compact In complete metric spaces, a set is compact if and only if it is closed and totally bounded (\cite{munkres_topology_2014 Theorem 45.1).}
elements.sent-2411-168: Totally bounded means for a subset $K$ of a metric space that for all $\epsilon >0$, there is a finite collection of open balls of radius $\epsilon$ whose union contains $K$.
elements.sent-2411-169: If for a subset $K$ of a normed vector space $T$, for all $\epsilon >0$ there is a finite-dimensional subspace $F_\epsilon \subset T$ and a bounded set $L_\epsilon \subset F_\epsilon$ such that any element of $K$ is at most $\epsilon$ away from an element of $L_\epsilon$, then $K$ is totally bounded for the following reason: For a given $\epsilon>0$ we want to show that $K$ can be covered by finitely many open balls of radius $\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-170: Now take the finite dimensional subspace and the bounded set therein for $\frac{\epsilon {2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-171: We know that $L_{\frac{\epsilon {2 }$ can be covered by finitely many open balls of radius $\frac{\epsilon {2}$ because it is a bounded set in a finite-dimensional vector space (so its closure is compact).}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Denote the center points of these finitely many open $\frac{\epsilon {2}$-balls by $x_1,\hdots,x_r$.}
elements.sent-2411-173: We show that the collection of $\epsilon$-balls around $x_1,\ldots,x_r$ covers $K$.
